teachingSummary:
**Core Rule:** A unilateral mistake occurs where one party is mistaken about a fundamental term of the contract and the other party knows or ought to know of the mistake. Unlike common mistake (which voids the contract at common law), unilateral mistake generally renders the contract voidable in equity — or void at common law in the narrow category of identity mistakes.

**The Identity Cases:** The classic unilateral mistake scenario is where A contracts with B, believing B to be C. If A is mistaken as to B's identity and this is fundamental to the contract, the contract may be void. The key question is whether the contract was made inter praesentes (face-to-face) or at a distance (by correspondence).

**Face-to-Face Presumption:** Where parties deal face-to-face, there is a strong presumption that A intends to contract with the person physically present, regardless of what name that person gives. In *Phillips v Brooks* [1919] 2 KB 243, a rogue posing as 'Sir George Bullough' bought jewellery by cheque (which bounced). The rogue then sold a ring to a pawnbroker. The court held the jeweller contracted with the person in the shop — the rogue — and the contract was voidable for fraudulent misrepresentation, not void. As the contract had not been rescinded before the pawnbroker bought the ring, the pawnbroker acquired good title under the nemo dat exception.

**In short:** Where the mistake is only one party's and relates to identity, the court must determine with whom the mistaken party intended to contract. Face-to-face dealings raise a strong presumption that the contract is with the person physically present.

question: In __________ v Lindsay [1878] 3 App Cas 459, a written contract with a rogue who signed as 'Blenkarn & Co' was held void because Blenkarn & Co did not exist — the claimants intended to contract only with Blenkiron & Co, a reputable firm they knew.
feedback: Correct. *Cundy v Lindsay* [1878] — the rogue intercepted correspondence and posed as 'Blenkarn & Co,' which the claimants mistook for 'Blenkiron & Co.' As the contract was made by correspondence (not face-to-face), the identity of the contracting party mattered. The contract was void for unilateral mistake as to identity.

question: Under section ____ of the Sale of Goods Act 1979, where a contract is voidable but has not been rescinded before a third party acquires the goods in good faith and for value, the third party obtains good title — a key factor in the identity mistake cases.
feedback: Correct. SGA 1979, s.23 — the 'voidable title' exception to nemo dat. If the seller's title is voidable (e.g. for fraud) and they sell before rescission, a good faith purchaser for value without notice acquires good title.

question: Match each case to the consequence of the mistake as to identity:
answer: Cundy v Lindsay [1878]=Contract held void — identity fundamental, written contract; Phillips v Brooks [1919]=Contract voidable — face-to-face, contracted with person present; Lewis v Averay [1972]=Contract voidable — face-to-face with rogue impersonator

options.text: Contract held void — identity was fundamental, written correspondence
Contract voidable — face-to-face dealing, contracted with person present
Contract voidable — face-to-face with a rogue impersonating a well-known actor

feedback: Well matched. The critical distinction is between void (identity fundamental, written/distance contract) and voidable (face-to-face, contracted with the person in the room).

question: A rogue enters a gallery and says, 'I am Lord Ashworth' — a wealthy collector known to the gallery owner by reputation. The owner sells a £20,000 painting, accepting a cheque (which bounces). The rogue sells the painting to a bona fide purchaser before the cheque bounces. Applying *Phillips v Brooks* [1919]:

options.text: The contract was void for mistake as to identity — the gallery owner never intended to contract with the rogue
options.feedback: Incorrect. The parties dealt face-to-face. The presumption is that the gallery owner intended to contract with the person standing in the gallery.

options.text: The gallery owner can recover the painting from the innocent purchaser because title never passed
options.feedback: Incorrect. If the contract is voidable (not void), and it has not been rescinded before the sale to the bona fide purchaser, SGA 1979, s.23 protects the innocent purchaser.

options.text: The gallery owner can rescind and recover the painting from the innocent purchaser
options.feedback: Incorrect. Rescission must occur before the third party acquires the goods. The bona fide purchaser is protected under SGA 1979, s.23.

options.text: The contract was voidable for fraudulent misrepresentation, but the gallery owner lost the right to rescind because the rogue sold the painting before rescission to a bona fide purchaser — who obtains good title
options.feedback: Correct. *Phillips v Brooks* [1919]: face-to-face dealing creates a presumption that the seller intends to contract with the person present. The contract is voidable for fraud, but once a bona fide purchaser acquires the goods before rescission, title passes. The gallery owner's remedy is against the rogue (if they can find them).

feedback: This is the *Phillips v Brooks* [1919] scenario. The face-to-face presumption is critical: the gallery owner intended to contract with the person in the gallery. The fraud makes the contract voidable, but rescission must happen before third-party rights intervene. SGA 1979, s.23 protects the innocent purchaser.

question:
Spot the legal error:

'If a rogue lies about their identity to buy goods, the contract is always void for unilateral mistake. The seller can recover the goods from anyone, because a void contract transfers no title under the nemo dat principle.'
answer: Not all identity fraud makes a contract void. In face-to-face dealings, the contract is generally voidable (not void) — the seller intended to contract with the person present. If the contract is voidable and has not been rescinded before sale to an innocent third party, the third party acquires good title under SGA 1979, s.23.
feedback: Found it. The distinction between void and voidable is central. *Cundy v Lindsay* [1878] (void) involved written correspondence where identity was everything. *Phillips v Brooks* [1919] (voidable) involved face-to-face dealing where the person's physical presence was enough. The consequence for third-party purchasers is significant.

question: In which TWO of the following scenarios is a contract most likely to be **void** (not voidable) for unilateral mistake as to identity?

options.text: A rogue sends a letter ordering goods, signing as a well-known firm with whom the seller has previously dealt — the contract is made entirely by correspondence
options.feedback: Correct. *Cundy v Lindsay* [1878] — written contracts made at a distance where identity is fundamental can be void.

options.text: A rogue enters a shop, claims to be a famous actor, and buys jewellery with a bad cheque — the sale is face-to-face
options.feedback: Face-to-face dealings create a presumption the seller intended to contract with the person present. The contract is voidable, not void.

options.text: A rogue buys a car in person using a false name but pays with a valid bank transfer — the seller checks the rogue's driving licence
options.feedback: The seller contracted with the person present. The false name alone does not make the contract void — it is likely voidable for fraud if the payment was dishonest.

options.text: A rogue impersonates a Ltd company in a written hire-purchase agreement, and the finance company relies exclusively on the rogue's written representations
options.feedback: Correct. *Shogun Finance v Hudson* [2003] — where the finance company deals exclusively in writing with an identified entity (via the rogue's use of stolen documents), the contract can be void. The written identity is fundamental.

options.text: A rogue attends an auction, bids under their own name using a stolen credit card, and collects the goods
options.feedback: The auctioneer contracted with the person at the auction. Face-to-face dealings create a voidable, not void, contract.

feedback: Correct answers: (a) and (d). Written/distance contracts where the counterparty's written identity is fundamental to the decision to contract can be void (*Cundy v Lindsay* [1878], *Shogun Finance* [2003]). Face-to-face dealings are almost always voidable, not void.
oscoaReferences: Shogun Finance v Hudson [2003] UKHL 62, [2004] 1 AC 919

question: The case of __________ v Brooks [1919] 2 KB 243 established the principle that in face-to-face dealings, there is a strong presumption that the seller intends to contract with the person physically present.
feedback: Correct. *Phillips v Brooks* [1919] — the rogue posed as 'Sir George Bullough' in the jeweller's shop. The contract was voidable for fraud, not void for mistake.

teachingSummary:
**Core Rule:** *Lewis v Averay* [1972] 1 QB 198 is the leading modern authority on identity mistakes in face-to-face transactions. The Court of Appeal confirmed that where parties contract inter praesentes, the contract is voidable (not void) — and the protection of innocent third-party purchasers is paramount.

**The Facts:** A rogue visited Mr Lewis, who was selling his car, and claimed to be Richard Greene — a well-known actor who played Robin Hood on television. The rogue examined the car, wrote a cheque for £450 signed 'R.A. Green,' and asked for proof of identity. The rogue produced a film studio pass with the name 'Richard Greene' and a photograph. Mr Lewis, satisfied, handed over the car and logbook. The cheque bounced. The rogue sold the car to Mr Averay, a bona fide purchaser.

**The Decision:** The Court of Appeal held that Mr Lewis intended to contract with the person physically present before him — the rogue. The contract was voidable for fraudulent misrepresentation, not void for mistake. As Mr Lewis had not rescinded the contract before the rogue sold to Mr Averay, Mr Averay acquired good title under SGA 1979, s.23. The loss fell on Mr Lewis, who was in a better position to verify identity.

**Lord Denning's Analysis:** The contract was with the person in the room — the rogue — not with the real Richard Greene. The identity was not fundamental because Mr Lewis dealt face-to-face and had every opportunity to check. The rogue's fraud did not prevent formation of a voidable contract.

**In short:** Face-to-face dealings are presumed to be with the person present. The contract is voidable for fraud but stands until rescinded. If a bona fide purchaser acquires the goods before rescission, they take good title.

question: In __________ v Averay [1972] 1 QB 198, the Court of Appeal held that a face-to-face sale to a rogue impersonating a television actor was voidable, not void — the seller intended to contract with the person present.
feedback: Correct. *Lewis v Averay* [1972] — Lord Denning MR held that the seller contracted with the person standing before him, not with the real Richard Greene. The rogue's fraud gave rise to a voidable contract, and the innocent purchaser (Mr Averay) got good title.

question: In *Lewis v Averay* [1972], the innocent purchaser was protected because the original contract was __________ (not void), and rescission had not occurred before the sale to the third party.
feedback: Correct. The voidable/void distinction is critical. If voidable and not yet rescinded, SGA 1979, s.23 gives the bona fide purchaser good title. If void, no title passes and the original owner can recover.

question: Place the facts of *Lewis v Averay* [1972] in chronological order:
answer: Rogue views car and claims to be actor Richard Greene → Rogue writes cheque for £450 and shows studio pass as ID → Mr Lewis hands over car and logbook → Cheque bounces — rogue has no connection to Richard Greene → Rogue sells car to bona fide purchaser Mr Averay → Mr Lewis sues Mr Averay for return of car — loses

options.text: Rogue views car and claims to be the actor Richard Greene
Rogue writes cheque for £450 and shows a film studio pass as identity proof
Mr Lewis hands over the car and logbook
Cheque bounces — rogue has no connection to the real Richard Greene
Rogue sells car to bona fide purchaser Mr Averay
Mr Lewis sues Mr Averay for return of the car — claim fails

feedback: Correct. The Court of Appeal prioritised the protection of the innocent third party over the original owner who could have verified identity. Mr Lewis was in the best position to prevent the fraud.

question: Viktor sells his laptop to a stranger at a café who says, 'I'm Elon Musk's brother.' Viktor, impressed, hands over the laptop for a cheque that later bounces. The stranger sells the laptop to an innocent buyer the next day. Applying *Lewis v Averay* [1972]:

options.text: The contract is void — Viktor never intended to contract with the stranger
options.feedback: Incorrect. The transaction was face-to-face. Under *Lewis v Averay* [1972], Viktor intended to contract with the person at the café.

options.text: The contract is voidable for fraud, but the innocent buyer acquires good title because Viktor did not rescind before the resale
options.feedback: Correct. This mirrors *Lewis v Averay* [1972]. Viktor contracted with the person present. The contract was voidable for fraud, but as rescission did not occur before the sale to the innocent buyer, SGA 1979, s.23 protects the buyer.

options.text: Viktor can recover the laptop from the innocent buyer because title was void and never passed
options.feedback: Incorrect. The contract is voidable, not void — title passed (albeit voidably). The innocent buyer takes good title under SGA 1979, s.23.

options.text: Both Viktor and the innocent buyer share the loss equally
options.feedback: There is no apportionment rule. The loss falls on the party best placed to prevent the fraud — here, Viktor, who could have verified the stranger's identity.

feedback: Under *Lewis v Averay* [1972], the face-to-face transaction creates a voidable (not void) contract. The key fact is that the innocent buyer purchased before Viktor rescinded. The nemo dat principle yields to the SGA 1979, s.23 exception — the innocent buyer gets good title.

question:
Spot the legal error:

'Under *Lewis v Averay* [1972], if a rogue impersonates someone else and buys goods face-to-face with a bad cheque, the contract is void and the original owner can always recover the goods from any subsequent purchaser.'
answer: *Lewis v Averay* [1972] held the contract was **voidable**, not void. The loss fell on the original owner (Mr Lewis) because the innocent purchaser (Mr Averay) acquired good title before rescission. The owner cannot recover from a bona fide purchaser.
feedback: Found it. The error conflates void and voidable. *Lewis v Averay* [1972] is authority for the proposition that face-to-face identity fraud creates a voidable contract. The original owner loses if a bona fide purchaser buys before rescission — as Mr Lewis discovered.

question: Which TWO of the following correctly explain why Mr Lewis lost in *Lewis v Averay* [1972]?

options.text: The contract was face-to-face, so Mr Lewis was presumed to have intended to contract with the person physically present
options.feedback: Correct. The face-to-face presumption is the starting point.

options.text: The contract was void, but Mr Averay had already sold the car on
options.feedback: Incorrect. The contract was voidable, not void. This is the core distinction from *Cundy v Lindsay* [1878].

options.text: Mr Lewis had signed a written contract with a named entity
options.feedback: Incorrect. There was no written contract — the sale was face-to-face with a cheque and delivery.

options.text: The rogue sold the car to Mr Averay (a bona fide purchaser) before Mr Lewis could rescind — and SGA 1979, s.23 protects the innocent buyer
options.feedback: Correct. Timing of rescission is critical. Rescission must occur before third-party rights intervene.

options.text: Mr Lewis had been negligent in accepting the cheque
options.feedback: Mr Lewis actually asked for and checked identification — the court did not base its decision on negligence, but on the legal classification of the contract as voidable.

feedback: Correct answers: (a) and (d). The face-to-face presumption plus the intervention of a bona fide purchaser before rescission sealed Mr Lewis's fate. The case illustrates the policy choice: between two innocent parties, the one best placed to prevent the fraud bears the loss.

question: Ingrid places an online private seller advertisement for her vintage watch. A buyer emails, claiming to be 'Barclays Bank's procurement officer' and sends a company purchase order by email. Ingrid delivers the watch. The buyer is a fraud. The watch is sold to a pawn shop. Under *Cundy v Lindsay* [1878], could this contract be void (not voidable)?

options.text: No — all online sales are face-to-face equivalents and always voidable
options.feedback: Incorrect. Online/distance sales are not face-to-face. They are more analogous to written correspondence.

options.text: No — *Cundy v Lindsay* only applies to contracts before 1900
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Cundy v Lindsay* [1878] remains good law and applies to any written/distance contract where identity is fundamental.

options.text: Potentially yes — the contract was made at a distance in writing, and Ingrid relied on the written identity. Under *Cundy v Lindsay* [1878], if identity was fundamental, the contract could be void
options.feedback: Correct. The email correspondence is analogous to the letters in *Cundy v Lindsay* [1878]. Ingrid never met the buyer face-to-face. If she intended to contract *only* with Barclays Bank, the rogue's impersonation could make the contract void for unilateral mistake as to identity.

options.text: Yes — all contracts induced by fraud are void for mistake
options.feedback: Incorrect. Fraud generally makes contracts voidable, not void. Only identity mistakes in non-face-to-face dealings can render a contract void.

feedback: The distance/face-to-face distinction remains central. Email correspondence is akin to the written letters in *Cundy v Lindsay* [1878] — the parties are not physically present, so identity is inferred from the writing. If Ingrid intended to contract with a specific entity, the rogue's impersonation could void the contract.

teachingSummary:
**Core Rule:** *Shogun Finance v Hudson* [2003] UKHL 62, [2004] 1 AC 919 is the House of Lords' most recent authority on unilateral mistake as to identity. The Lords divided 3:2, but the majority confirmed that where a contract is made entirely in writing with a named party, and the rogue impersonates that party by producing stolen documents, the contract is void — not just voidable.

**The Facts:** A rogue visited a car dealership, selected a Mitsubishi Shogun, and offered to buy it on hire-purchase. The dealer faxed a proposal form to Shogun Finance, which conducted a credit check on the person named on the stolen driving licence the rogue produced. The finance company approved the credit agreement — the contract on its face was between Shogun Finance and the named individual (the real person on the licence). The rogue paid a deposit and drove the car away, then sold it to Mr Hudson, an innocent purchaser.

**The Decision (3:2):** The majority (Lords Hobhouse, Phillips, and Walker) held that the contract was void. Shogun Finance had contracted only with the named individual (the real licence holder), not with the rogue. The contract was entirely in writing and the written identity was the only means of identification. There was no face-to-face dealing with the finance company — only with the dealer (who was not the creditor). Lord Nicholls and Lord Millett dissented, preferring to treat the contract as voidable on policy grounds.

**The Principle:** Where a contract is made entirely in writing with an identified party, and the identity of that party is fundamental to the agreement, a rogue's impersonation can render the contract void for unilateral mistake. The written name on the contract determines the counterparty.

**In short:** *Shogun Finance* divides identity cases along face-to-face (voidable — *Lewis v Averay*) and written/distance (void — *Cundy v Lindsay*, *Shogun*) lines.
oscoaReferences: Shogun Finance v Hudson [2003] UKHL 62, [2004] 1 AC 919

question: In __________ Finance v Hudson [2003] UKHL 62, the House of Lords held (3:2) that a hire-purchase agreement was void for unilateral mistake because the finance company intended to contract only with the named individual on the credit documents.
feedback: Correct. *Shogun Finance v Hudson* [2003] — the rogue used a stolen driving licence. The finance company's contract was with the person named on the licence, not the rogue. The contract was void.
oscoaReferences: Shogun Finance v Hudson [2003] UKHL 62, [2004] 1 AC 919

question: Under the nemo dat rule, a person cannot transfer __________ title than they themselves possess. The SGA 1979 contains limited exceptions, including s.23 (voidable title).
feedback: Correct. Nemo dat quod non habet — no one can give what they do not have. If the original contract is void, the rogue never acquires title and cannot pass it. If voidable, the rogue has voidable title and can pass good title to a bona fide purchaser before rescission (SGA 1979, s.23).

question: Match each case to its key ratio on mistake as to identity:
answer: Cundy v Lindsay [1878]=Written contract with impersonated firm — void; Phillips v Brooks [1919]=Face-to-face sale — voidable, innocent purchaser protected; Lewis v Averay [1972]=Face-to-face sale with ID check — voidable, innocent purchaser protected; Shogun Finance [2003]=Written hire-purchase with named individual — void

options.text: Written correspondence — rogue impersonated a known firm — contract void
Face-to-face sale — rogue posed as a baronet — contract voidable
Face-to-face sale — rogue showed fake studio pass — contract voidable
Written hire-purchase — rogue used stolen driving licence — contract void

feedback: Well matched. The line between void (written/distance) and voidable (face-to-face) cases is the organising principle of this area. *Shogun* [2003] is the most recent and most controversial application.
oscoaReferences: Shogun Finance v Hudson [2003] UKHL 62, [2004] 1 AC 919

question: A car finance company receives a faxed application in the name 'John Smith' with a stolen driving licence and payslip. The company approves the finance without ever meeting the applicant. The rogue collects the car from the dealer and sells it to an innocent buyer. Applying *Shogun Finance* [2003] majority reasoning:

options.text: The contract is void — the finance company intended to contract with the real John Smith named in the documents, not the rogue. The written contract was with the named individual only
options.feedback: Correct. The majority in *Shogun Finance* [2003] held that where the contract is entirely in writing with an identified individual, and identity is fundamental, the rogue's impersonation makes the contract void. The finance company never contracted with the rogue.

options.text: The contract is voidable only — the rogue collected the car face-to-face from the dealer
options.feedback: Incorrect. The face-to-face dealing with the dealer is separate from the finance contract. The finance company dealt entirely in writing with the named individual — the majority in *Shogun* distinguished the dealer interaction.

options.text: The contract is voidable, but the finance company can still recover the car from the innocent buyer
options.feedback: Incorrect. If the contract were voidable (not void), and the rogue sold before rescission, the innocent buyer would be protected under SGA 1979, s.23.

options.text: The finance company's claim fails because it was negligent in checking the rogue's identity
options.feedback: The majority in *Shogun* did not base their decision on the finance company's negligence — they focused on the formation of the contract and with whom the contract was made.

feedback: This is the *Shogun Finance* [2003] scenario. The majority reasoned that the finance company's contract was a written contract with the named individual. The rogue was not that individual. The contract was therefore void. Note the 3:2 split — a differently constituted court might have reached the opposite conclusion.
oscoaReferences: Shogun Finance v Hudson [2003] UKHL 62, [2004] 1 AC 919

question:
Spot the legal error:

'*Shogun Finance v Hudson* [2003] overruled *Lewis v Averay* [1972] and established that all identity mistakes make a contract void. The face-to-face distinction is no longer relevant.'
answer: *Shogun* [2003] did NOT overrule *Lewis v Averay* [1972]. The House of Lords distinguished (not overruled) the face-to-face cases. The face-to-face vs written contract distinction remains the organising principle. *Lewis v Averay* is still good law for face-to-face dealings.
feedback: Found it. *Shogun* [2003] distinguished — but did not overrule — *Lewis v Averay* [1972]. The majority emphasised that their decision applied to written contracts where the finance company never dealt face-to-face with the rogue. *Lewis v Averay* continues to govern face-to-face identity mistakes.
oscoaReferences: Shogun Finance v Hudson [2003] UKHL 62, [2004] 1 AC 919

question: Which TWO of the following correctly state the law after *Shogun Finance v Hudson* [2003]?

options.text: All contracts induced by identity fraud are void, regardless of whether they are face-to-face or written
options.feedback: Incorrect. Face-to-face dealings remain voidable (*Lewis v Averay* [1972] is still good law).

options.text: Where a contract is made entirely in writing with an identified named party, an impersonation can render the contract void
options.feedback: Correct. This is the *Shogun* [2003] ratio — the written identity defines the contracting party.

options.text: The distinction between face-to-face dealings (voidable) and written/distance dealings (void) remains the organising principle
options.feedback: Correct. Despite criticism, the face-to-face/written distinction survives and was reaffirmed by the majority in *Shogun*.

options.text: Lord Nicholls' dissenting judgment in *Shogun Finance* represents the law
options.feedback: Incorrect. Lord Nicholls' dissent (and Lord Millett's) favoured treating all such contracts as voidable. The majority judgment represents the law, although it has been criticised.

options.text: The nemo dat rule no longer applies to sale of goods contracts
options.feedback: Incorrect. Nemo dat remains fundamental. SGA 1979, s.23 is the exception, not the rule.

feedback: Correct answers: (b) and (c). The *Shogun* [2003] majority (3:2) preserved the face-to-face/written distinction and held that written contracts with identified individuals can be void for unilateral mistake. The judgment has been subject to academic criticism but remains authoritative.
oscoaReferences: Shogun Finance v Hudson [2003] UKHL 62, [2004] 1 AC 919

question: A different kind of unilateral mistake: BuildCo Ltd tenders for a construction project. Due to a spreadsheet error, it omits a £200,000 sub-contractor cost. CompCo, the employer, notices the error in the pricing schedule but says nothing and accepts the tender. BuildCo discovers the error and refuses to proceed at the tender price. CompCo argues BuildCo is bound. Applying the principle that a contract may be voidable for unilateral mistake where one party knows of the other's error:

options.text: BuildCo is bound — unilateral mistakes never affect contractual validity
options.feedback: Incorrect. Where one party knows of the other's mistake and snaps at the bargain, equity may intervene.

options.text: BuildCo can claim common mistake because both parties made an error about the price
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a unilateral mistake — only BuildCo was mistaken. CompCo knew of the error. Common mistake requires both parties to share the mistake.

options.text: The contract is voidable — CompCo had actual knowledge of BuildCo's serious error and took unfair advantage. BuildCo can seek rescission
options.feedback: Correct. A unilateral mistake may render a contract voidable where the non-mistaken party knew of the error and sought to take advantage. This is distinct from the identity cases and is based on equitable fraud.

options.text: BuildCo must perform at the mistaken price — its own negligence in pricing is no defence
options.feedback: Incorrect. While BuildCo was careless, CompCo's knowledge of the error changes the analysis. Equity does not permit a party to knowingly exploit another's unilateral mistake.

feedback: Unilateral mistake is not limited to identity cases. Where one party makes a serious pricing error and the other knows of it and seeks to take advantage (sometimes called 'snapping up a bargain'), the contract may be voidable in equity. The non-mistaken party's knowledge is the key factor.
oscoaReferences: Hartog v Colin & Shields [1939] 3 All ER 566
OTV Birwelco v Technical & General Guarantee [2002] EWHC 2240 (TCC)

question: Match the type of unilateral mistake to its consequence:
answer: Mistake as to identity (face-to-face)=Contract voidable — fraud; identity lost if carelessness or third-party rights; Mistake as to identity (written/distance)=Contract void — identity fundamental, no contract with rogue; Mistake as to terms (known to other party)=Contract voidable — 'snapping up' a known mistake; Mistake as to quality or value (unknown to other)=No relief — caveat emptor

options.text: Contract voidable for fraud — third-party rights may defeat rescission
Contract void — no contract ever formed with the impersonator
Contract voidable — other party's knowledge of the error makes enforcement unconscionable
options.pair: Mistake as to terms (known to other party)

options.text: No relief — the law does not protect against one's own poor judgment
options.pair: Mistake as to quality or value (unknown to other)

feedback: Well matched. Unilateral mistake covers a spectrum from void (written identity), through voidable (face-to-face identity, known mistake of terms), to no relief (unknown mistake of quality). Knowledge of the other party is the critical variable.
oscoaReferences: Shogun Finance v Hudson [2003] UKHL 62, [2004] 1 AC 919
Hartog v Colin & Shields [1939] 3 All ER 566

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block A] An art dealer receives a letter on headed paper: 'Messrs Blenkinsop & Co, Fine Art Dealers, Mayfair' offering to buy a painting on credit. The dealer ships the painting to the Mayfair address. The letter was written by a rogue — Blenkinsop & Co is a real firm but knew nothing of the order. The rogue sells the painting. Applying *Cundy v Lindsay* [1878]:

options.text: The contract is voidable — the dealer should have checked the fraud more carefully
options.feedback: The dealer was deceived by correspondence, not face-to-face. *Cundy v Lindsay* [1878] controls.

options.text: The contract is void — the dealer intended to contract with Blenkinsop & Co only. The written correspondence makes identity fundamental. The contract was with no one
options.feedback: Correct. This mirrors *Cundy v Lindsay* [1878]. The rogue's written impersonation of a known firm was fundamental. The dealer never intended to contract with the rogue. The contract is void.

options.text: The dealer can recover the painting from the innocent purchaser because the contract is voidable and has been rescinded
options.feedback: If the contract is void (not voidable), no rescission is needed — title never passed. The dealer can claim the painting from anyone.

options.text: The contract is valid — the dealer assumed the risk of dealing by post
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Cundy v Lindsay* [1878] expressly held that written impersonation can make a contract void. The dealer did not assume the risk of fraud.

feedback: This consolidates Block A and the *Cundy v Lindsay* [1878] distance-contract principle. Where identity is conveyed only in writing and is fundamental to the decision to contract, the rogue's impersonation renders the contract void. The painting can be recovered even from an innocent third party.

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block B] In Lewis v __________ [1972] 1 QB 198, the Court of Appeal held that in a face-to-face sale to a rogue impersonator, the contract is voidable (not void) for fraudulent misrepresentation.
feedback: Correct. *Lewis v Averay* [1972] — the rogue posed as Richard Greene. The Court held the seller contracted with the person present and the contract was voidable only. The innocent purchaser kept the car.

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block B] Yasmin sells her motorbike to a man at her front door who says, 'I'm David Beckham.' He writes a cheque for £8,000 and shows a passport that matches the name. He rides away. The cheque bounces. He sells the bike to an innocent buyer the same afternoon. Applying *Lewis v Averay* [1972] and *Phillips v Brooks* [1919]:

options.text: Yasmin can recover the bike from the innocent buyer because the contract is void
options.feedback: Face-to-face dealings with identity fraud create voidable, not void, contracts.

options.text: Yasmin can recover because she checked the passport, making the mistake fundamental
options.feedback: Checking ID does not convert a face-to-face contract into a distance contract. The face-to-face presumption still applies.

options.text: Yasmin loses — the contract was face-to-face and voidable, and the innocent buyer purchased before rescission
options.feedback: Correct. Under *Lewis v Averay* [1972], the face-to-face transaction created a voidable contract. The innocent buyer purchased before rescission, acquiring good title under SGA 1979, s.23.

options.text: Yasmin can rescind the contract and compel the buyer to return the bike
options.feedback: Rescission must occur before third-party rights intervene. The innocent buyer bought the bike the same afternoon — before any rescission. SGA 1979, s.23 protects the buyer.

feedback: The face-to-face principle is determinative. Despite checking ID, Yasmin contracted with the person at her door. The contract was voidable. The innocent buyer's intervening purchase defeats rescission. As between two innocents, the law protects the final purchaser.

question: [CONSOLIDATION — All Blocks] Which TWO of the following correctly describe the approach to unilateral mistake as to identity in English law?

options.text: Face-to-face dealings are presumed to be with the person physically present — the contract is voidable for fraud, not void
options.feedback: Correct. *Lewis v Averay* [1972] and *Phillips v Brooks* [1919] — the face-to-face presumption makes the contract voidable.

options.text: All identity mistakes render the contract void, and the original owner can always recover goods from third parties
options.feedback: Incorrect. Only written/distance contracts can be void. Face-to-face contracts are voidable.

options.text: The face-to-face vs written distinction was abolished by *Shogun Finance* [2003]
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Shogun* [2003] confirmed the distinction — it distinguished, not overruled, the face-to-face cases.

options.text: In *Cundy v Lindsay* [1878], the face-to-face contract was held voidable
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Cundy v Lindsay* [1878] was a written/distance contract held void — there was no face-to-face dealing.

options.text: Where a contract is in writing with a named party, the written identity defines the counterparty, and impersonation can void the contract
options.feedback: Correct. *Shogun Finance* [2003] — the written contract was with the named individual, not the rogue.

feedback: Correct answers: (a) and (e). The organising principle is the mode of contracting: face-to-face (voidable) vs written/distance (potentially void). Both lines of authority survive and operate in different contexts.
oscoaReferences: Shogun Finance v Hudson [2003] UKHL 62, [2004] 1 AC 919

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block C] An online lender approves a car loan application submitted through a dealer's online portal. The application names 'Aisha Khan' and uses her stolen identity documents. The lender approves the loan. The car is collected and sold to an innocent buyer. The real Aisha Khan knows nothing of the transaction. Applying *Shogun Finance* [2003] to the online context:

options.text: The contract is void — the lender contracted with the identity 'Aisha Khan' in the written application. The rogue was not Aisha Khan. The innocent buyer cannot acquire title because the rogue had none to pass
options.feedback: Correct. The online application is a written/distance contract — no face-to-face interaction with the lender. Under *Shogun Finance* [2003], the contract is with the named individual. The contract is void.

options.text: The contract is voidable because the dealer met the rogue face-to-face
options.feedback: The dealer's face-to-face interaction is not the lender's contract. *Shogun* [2003] distinguishes the two.

options.text: The lender is bound by the contract because online applications are always face-to-face equivalents
options.feedback: Online written applications are not face-to-face. They are distance contracts — identity is conveyed in writing only.

options.text: The contract is void, but the innocent buyer can keep the car on policy grounds
options.feedback: If the contract is void, the rogue never had title. Nemo dat applies in full — the innocent buyer cannot claim title through a void contract.

feedback: The online application is the written/distance mode of contracting. Under *Shogun Finance* [2003] majority reasoning, the lender's contract is with the named identity in writing. The rogue's impersonation renders the contract void. This outcome (protecting the lender over the innocent buyer) has been criticised but represents the current law.
oscoaReferences: Shogun Finance v Hudson [2003] UKHL 62, [2004] 1 AC 919

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block A] The case of Cundy v __________ [1878] 3 App Cas 459 established that a contract made by correspondence can be void for unilateral mistake as to identity where the claimant intended to contract only with a particular firm.
feedback: Correct. *Cundy v Lindsay* [1878] — the House of Lords held the contract void. The rogue's written impersonation of Blenkiron & Co was fundamental; the claimants never intended to deal with the rogue.
